title: Security & Control
simple_html:
Information security means protecting data and systems from harm; controls are the policies and tools that keep them safe. The classic goal is the CIA triad: keep data Confidential (only the right people see it), keep it with Integrity (it stays accurate and unaltered), and keep it Available (it is there when needed).

Why it matters

A single breach can cost millions, halt operations, leak customers' private data, and destroy trust. As businesses move online and to the cloud, more doors exist for attackers, so security is now a board-level business issue, not just an IT chore.

Common threats (in plain terms)

  • Malware — harmful software like viruses and ransomware (which locks your files until you pay).
  • Phishing — fake emails or messages that trick people into giving passwords or clicking bad links.
  • Social engineering — manipulating people, the weakest link, rather than machines.
  • Denial of service — flooding a system so it crashes and becomes unavailable.
  • Insider threats — employees who misuse access, accidentally or on purpose.

Key defenses

  • Authentication — proving who you are; multi-factor authentication (MFA) uses two or more proofs (password plus a phone code).
  • Encryption — scrambling data so only authorized people can read it.
  • Firewalls and antivirus — blocking and detecting attacks.
  • Backups — copies that let you recover after ransomware or disaster.
  • Defense in depth — many layers, so if one fails others still protect you.

Key takeaway: aim for the CIA triad, expect human error, and layer your defenses. Security is about managing risk, not achieving perfection.
intermediate_html:
Security and control is the discipline of protecting an organization's information assets and ensuring systems behave as intended. You should be able to state the core objective, classify threats and vulnerabilities, and select appropriate controls using a risk-based approach.

The objective: the CIA triad

All security work serves three goals. Confidentiality restricts access to authorized parties. Integrity ensures data is accurate and unmodified except by authorized processes. Availability guarantees authorized users can access systems when needed. Many breaches map cleanly to one corner: a data leak harms confidentiality, fraudulent record changes harm integrity, and ransomware or a DDoS attack harms availability.

Vulnerabilities, threats, and risk

A vulnerability is a weakness; a threat is a potential cause of harm; risk is the likelihood of a threat exploiting a vulnerability times the impact. Networked and cloud systems enlarge the attack surface, and the human layer (errors, weak passwords, susceptibility to social engineering) is often the easiest target. Major threat categories include malware (viruses, worms, ransomware), phishing, SQL injection, man-in-the-middle attacks, denial-of-service, and insider misuse.

Categories of control

TypePurposeExamples
PreventiveStop incidentsAccess control, MFA, encryption, firewalls
DetectiveSpot incidentsLogging, intrusion detection, audits
CorrectiveRecover after incidentsBackups, disaster recovery, patching

Controls are also split into general controls (govern the whole IT environment — software, physical, data, administrative) and application controls (specific to one system, e.g., input validation, processing, and output checks).

Key technical controls

Authentication verifies identity using factors: something you know (password), have (token/phone), or are (biometric). Combining factors yields multi-factor authentication (MFA), dramatically reducing account takeover. Encryption protects data at rest and in transit; symmetric encryption uses one shared key (fast), while asymmetric uses a public/private key pair enabling secure exchange and digital signatures. A firewall filters traffic between networks; defense in depth layers multiple controls so no single failure is catastrophic.

Worked example: risk-based prioritization

A mid-sized retailer lists three risks. (1) Ransomware: likelihood high, impact severe — high risk; mitigate with offline, tested backups, patching, MFA, and user training. (2) Stolen laptop: likelihood medium, impact medium — mitigate with full-disk encryption and remote wipe. (3) Insider data export: likelihood low, impact high — mitigate with least-privilege access, logging, and data-loss prevention. By scoring likelihood multiplied by impact, the firm spends first on the highest-risk items rather than the loudest one. This is the essence of risk assessment.

Governance and continuity

Security is also organizational. A security policy sets the rules; an acceptable use policy governs employees. Disaster recovery plans restore IT after an outage, while business continuity keeps the whole business operating. Frameworks like COBIT and standards like ISO 27001 structure IT governance and demonstrate due care to regulators and auditors.

How to apply it

  • Frame every issue against the CIA triad to clarify what is at stake.
  • Compute risk = likelihood x impact and prioritize accordingly.
  • Match each risk to preventive, detective, and corrective controls — layer them.
  • Remember the human factor: training and MFA often beat buying more hardware.
full_html:
Security and control is the management discipline of protecting information assets and ensuring information systems operate reliably, lawfully, and as intended. It spans technology, process, and people, and it is fundamentally an exercise in risk management under uncertainty rather than the pursuit of absolute safety. This lesson develops the objectives, threat landscape, control architecture, cryptographic foundations, governance frameworks, and the strategic and ethical stakes that follow.

Objectives: the CIA triad and beyond

The canonical objective is the CIA triad — confidentiality, integrity, and availability (Saltzer & Schroeder, 1975). Confidentiality limits disclosure; integrity preserves accuracy and trustworthiness; availability ensures timely access. Extensions add authenticity, accountability, and non-repudiation (the inability to deny an action), captured in models such as the Parkerian Hexad (Parker, 1998). These objectives translate directly into design requirements and into the criteria used to evaluate any control.

The threat landscape

A precise vocabulary matters: an asset has value; a vulnerability is a weakness; a threat is a circumstance that could exploit it; an exploit is the method; and risk is the expected loss, conventionally risk = likelihood x impact. Threats range from malware (viruses, worms, Trojans, and especially ransomware, which attacks availability and increasingly confidentiality via double extortion), to phishing and broader social engineering that target the human layer, to technical attacks such as SQL injection, cross-site scripting, man-in-the-middle interception, zero-day exploits, advanced persistent threats, and distributed denial-of-service. Mobility, cloud computing, BYOD, and the Internet of Things expand the attack surface, while supply-chain compromises (e.g., poisoned software updates) show that trust boundaries extend beyond the firm.

A control architecture

Controls are classified by function — preventive (block incidents), detective (identify them), and corrective (recover) — and by scope. General controls govern the whole IT environment: software controls, hardware/physical controls, data security controls, implementation controls, and administrative controls. Application controls are embedded in individual systems as input, processing, and output controls (Laudon & Laudon, 2022). The guiding principle is defense in depth: layered, redundant safeguards so that the failure of any one control is not catastrophic, complemented by least privilege and separation of duties (Saltzer & Schroeder, 1975) to limit both error and abuse.

Identity and access management

Authentication rests on factors — knowledge (password), possession (token, phone), and inherence (biometric) — and combining independent factors gives multi-factor authentication, which neutralizes most credential-theft attacks. Authorization then enforces what an authenticated identity may do, via models such as role-based access control. Strong, salted password hashing, account lockouts, and increasingly passwordless and FIDO2 methods reduce the dominant breach vector: stolen or weak credentials.

Cryptography

Encryption renders data unreadable without a key, protecting it at rest and in transit. Symmetric algorithms (e.g., AES) use one shared secret key — fast but requiring secure key exchange. Asymmetric (public-key) cryptography (Diffie & Hellman, 1976; Rivest, Shamir & Adleman, 1978) uses a mathematically linked public/private key pair, enabling secure key exchange, digital signatures, and non-repudiation. In practice systems combine both: asymmetric methods exchange a symmetric session key (as in TLS), and a public key infrastructure with digital certificates binds keys to identities, underpinning HTTPS and trusted e-commerce.

Governance, assurance, and continuity

Technology alone is insufficient; security must be governed. A security policy and acceptable use policy set expectations; IT governance frameworks such as COBIT, the NIST Cybersecurity Framework (Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover), and the ISO/IEC 27001 management-system standard provide structure and demonstrate due care to regulators. Resilience requires distinguishing disaster recovery (restoring IT after disruption, measured by recovery time objective and recovery point objective) from business continuity (keeping core business functions running). Regular backups — ideally following the 3-2-1 rule with at least one offline, tested copy — are the last line of defense against ransomware.

Worked example: quantifying and treating risk

Annualized loss expectancy formalizes prioritization: ALE = SLE x ARO, where single loss expectancy (SLE) = asset value x exposure factor, and ARO is the annual rate of occurrence. If a ransomware event would cause $500,000 in losses (SLE) and is expected once every two years (ARO = 0.5), then ALE = $250,000 per year. A $60,000 program of offline backups, MFA, and training that cuts the rate to once in ten years (ARO = 0.1) lowers ALE to $50,000 — a $200,000 annual risk reduction for $60,000, a clearly justified control. Management then chooses among the four risk responses: mitigate, transfer (cyber-insurance), accept, or avoid.

Nuances and debates

Several tensions define the field. Security versus usability: overly strict controls drive workarounds that create new risk. Privacy versus surveillance: monitoring that strengthens security can erode trust and run afoul of regulation (links to IS Ethics and GDPR). The economics of security are perverse — defenders must cover every vulnerability while attackers need only one (Anderson, 2001) — and breach disclosure, ransom payment ethics, and the offense/defense balance remain contested. Zero-trust architecture ("never trust, always verify") is reframing perimeter-based thinking for cloud and remote work.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Treating security as a technology purchase rather than an ongoing risk-management process.
  • Believing the firewall makes the network safe — ignoring the human layer and insider risk.
  • Confusing authentication (who you are) with authorization (what you may do).
  • Confusing disaster recovery with business continuity.
  • Keeping backups online and untested, so they are encrypted by the same ransomware they were meant to defeat.
  • Assuming compliance equals security — passing an audit is necessary but not sufficient.

Cross-links

This lesson follows directly from Networks, the Internet & E-commerce (every connected channel widens the attack surface) and feeds Building Systems & IS Ethics (security and privacy requirements must be designed in, not bolted on). It also intersects Data & Business Intelligence, since the value of data assets is precisely what makes their confidentiality and integrity worth defending.
